result:
If I were to write freely about something that stirs my mind in this moment, I’d write about silence—not as an absence of sound, but as a kind of presence.

Silence, when you truly meet it, has texture. It’s not empty. In the pause between breaths, in the stillness just before dawn, in the quiet of a room after someone leaves—silence hums. It holds echoes of what has been and whispers of what might be.

We often think of silence as something to fill—with music, conversation, notifications, the endless stream of our own thoughts. Yet there’s a deep and ancient kind of silence that listens back. It’s the silence of forests, of deep space, of holding a sleeping child. It’s in that quiet where we sometimes hear ourselves most clearly.

In a world that prizes noise—louder, faster, more—choosing silence can feel like a radical act. It’s not about shutting out the world, but tuning into a different frequency. A frequency where intuition can speak, where creativity can drift up from the depths, where the heart can untangle its own knots.

I think of the poets and mystics across cultures who have sought the quiet, who understood that some truths only settle when the noise settles. There’s a wisdom in stillness—a knowing that doesn’t come from words.

And perhaps that’s what draws me to it: silence is the original language. Before words, there was the quiet of being. It’s a place we can return to, a sanctuary we carry within, even amidst the chaos.

So here’s to the quiet moments—the spaces between. May we have the courage to step into them, to listen, and to hear what the silence has been trying to tell us all along.
